h1: Travel Spanish for Nicaragua
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Nicaragua?
intro: Nicaraguan Spanish uses voseo with 'vos' throughout. The 's' at the end of a syllable often becomes a breathy 'h'. Nicaraguans affectionately call themselves 'nicas' or 'pinoleros'. On the Caribbean coast, an English-based creole and indigenous languages such as Miskito are spoken.

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Nicaraguan Spanish uses voseo with 'vos' throughout. The 's' at the end of a syllable often becomes a breathy 'h'. Nicaraguans affectionately call themselves 'nicas' or 'pinoleros'. On the Caribbean coast, an English-based creole and indigenous languages such as Miskito are spoken.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Nicaragua is the largest country in Central America, known for the colonial gems of Granada and León and the vast Lake Nicaragua. Backpackers surf in San Juan del Sur, go volcano boarding on Cerro Negro and explore the island of Ometepe with its two volcanoes. Prices are low and the scenery spectacular.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before the conquest, peoples such as the Nicarao and Chorotega lived here. In 1524 Spain founded Granada and León. Nicaragua became independent in 1821. The 20th century was marked by the Somoza dictatorship and the Sandinista Revolution of 1979, whose consequences still shape politics today.

sections.heading: Country & people

One of the poorest countries in the hemisphere; a political crisis since 2018 weighs on the economy.
Agriculture (coffee, beef, sugar, gold), textile factories and remittances.

h1: Travel Spanish for Costa Rica
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Costa Rica?
intro: Costa Rican Spanish mostly uses 'usted', but also voseo with 'vos'. It sounds soft and polite, with its own slang. Its hallmark is 'Pura Vida' – greeting, thanks and a whole outlook on life all at once. Costa Ricans call themselves 'ticos'.

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Costa Rican Spanish mostly uses 'usted', but also voseo with 'vos'. It sounds soft and polite, with its own slang. Its hallmark is 'Pura Vida' – greeting, thanks and a whole outlook on life all at once. Costa Ricans call themselves 'ticos'.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Costa Rica is an ecotourism paradise with cloud forests, volcanoes and beaches on both the Pacific and the Caribbean. Around a quarter of the country is protected nature reserve, ideal for sloths, toucans and zip-lining. The laid-back 'Pura Vida' attitude is everywhere you go.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: The region was sparsely settled by indigenous peoples, without any great civilisation. Columbus landed on the Caribbean coast in 1502. After the Spanish colonial era, Costa Rica became independent in 1821. In 1948 the country abolished its army and invested in education and nature conservation instead.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 5.3 million inhabitants (2025).
A stable presidential democracy with no army of its own (abolished in 1948).
One of the wealthiest and most stable economies in Central America, with a strong middle class.
Eco-tourism, high-tech and medical-device exports, services as well as coffee and pineapple.

h1: Travel Spanish for Panama
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Panama?
intro: Panamanian Spanish sounds Caribbean: fast, with a swallowed 's' and soft endings. Voseo is unusual; people use 'tú'. Because of the canal's history, there are many anglicisms. Indigenous languages such as Guna and Ngäbere, as well as an English-based creole on the Caribbean coast, are common.

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Panamanian Spanish sounds Caribbean: fast, with a swallowed 's' and soft endings. Voseo is unusual; people use 'tú'. Because of the canal's history, there are many anglicisms. Indigenous languages such as Guna and Ngäbere, as well as an English-based creole on the Caribbean coast, are common.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: As a land bridge, Panama connects North and South America and is home to the world-famous Panama Canal. Backpackers are drawn to the Guna's idyllic San Blas Islands, the surf town of Bocas del Toro and the modern capital with its colonial old town. The Pacific and the Caribbean are just hours apart.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before the conquest, peoples such as the Guna, Emberá and Ngäbe lived here. The Spanish arrived in 1501, and Panama became an important transit point for silver. In 1821 the country broke away from Spain and joined Gran Colombia. In 1903 Panama gained independence from Colombia with US help; the canal opened in 1914 and was handed over to Panama in 1999.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 4.5 million inhabitants (2025).
A presidential republic; it uses the US dollar (balboa) as its currency.
One of the most dynamic economies in the region, driven by the canal and the financial sector.
The Panama Canal, logistics and ports, banking, trade in the Colón free-trade zone and tourism.

h1: Travel Spanish for Cuba
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Cuba?
intro: Cuban Spanish is fast and melodic, with the 's' at the end of a syllable often swallowed or softened to a breath (e.g. 'ehtá' instead of 'está'). The 'r' is frequently softened to 'l' too, and final syllables disappear. It's marked by strong Afro-Cuban influences and a lively tone full of…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Cuban Spanish is fast and melodic, with the 's' at the end of a syllable often swallowed or softened to a breath (e.g. 'ehtá' instead of 'está'). The 'r' is frequently softened to 'l' too, and final syllables disappear. It's marked by strong Afro-Cuban influences and a lively tone full of diminutives.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Cuba is the largest island in the Caribbean and captivates with colonial old towns, tobacco fields in the Viñales valley and miles of beaches like Varadero. Backpackers often travel by colectivo and stay in private casas particulares, which give an authentic glimpse into everyday life.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before Columbus arrived in 1492, the Taíno and Ciboney lived on the island, before Spain exploited Cuba as a colony and a centre of slavery for sugar cane. Spanish rule ended in 1898, but it was the Revolution of 1959 under Fidel Castro and Che Guevara that turned Cuba into a socialist state. After decades under the US embargo, the country is slowly opening up, but politically it remains a one-party state.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 11 million inhabitants (2025) – recently declining due to heavy emigration.
A socialist one-party state led by the Communist Party.
A state-run economy in deep crisis, with supply shortages and the US embargo.
Tourism, the export of doctors and medical services, tobacco (cigars), rum and nickel.

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Dominican Spanish is fast and heavily marked by the Caribbean accent, with the 's' swallowed at the end of a syllable (e.g. 'lo niño' instead of 'los niños'). The 'r' often becomes 'i' or 'l' (e.g. 'puelta' instead of 'puerta'), and many words are heavily shortened. There are numerous Taíno and African loanwords.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: The Dominican Republic shares the island of Hispaniola with Haiti and tempts you with palm-fringed beaches, the Pico Duarte mountain range and the historic Zona Colonial of Santo Domingo. Beyond the package resorts, backpackers also find authentic spots like Las Terrenas or the Damajagua waterfalls.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: The island was settled by the Taíno when Columbus landed in 1492, and Santo Domingo became the oldest permanent European settlement in the Americas. After Spanish and a brief Haitian rule, the country gained its independence from Haiti in 1844. The 20th century was marked by Trujillo's brutal dictatorship, before a democracy and a tourism-driven boom took shape.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 11.5 million inhabitants (2025).
A presidential republic with regular democratic elections.
One of the fastest-growing economies in Latin America.
Tourism (Punta Cana), free-trade zones, mining (gold), agriculture and remittances.

The 'r' at the start of a word is also often pronounced guttural, almost like a throaty French 'r'. Because of the US connection…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Puerto Rican Spanish has a Caribbean feel, with a swallowed 's' and the typical shift from 'r' to 'l' at the end of a syllable (e.g. 'puelto' instead of 'puerto'). The 'r' at the start of a word is also often pronounced guttural, almost like a throaty French 'r'. Because of the US connection, there are many English loanwords (Spanglish).

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Puerto Rico is a US territory with Caribbean flair, from cobblestoned Old San Juan to the El Yunque rainforest and the bioluminescent bays. As a backpacker you travel without a visa (if you're allowed into the US) and pay conveniently in US dollars.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: The Taíno called the island Borikén before Spain began colonising it in 1493 and heavily fortified San Juan. In 1898, after the Spanish-American War, Spain ceded Puerto Rico to the United States, whose citizens Puerto Ricans have been since 1917. Today the island is an unincorporated US territory with its own culture and an ongoing debate about its political status.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 3.2 million inhabitants (2025), with continuing migration to the US mainland.
An unincorporated US territory with its own governor; residents are US citizens.
On the US dollar; rebuilding after a debt crisis and Hurricane María.
Pharmaceutical and medical-device industry, tourism and services; tightly tied to the US economy.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Mofongo — A mash of fried plantains with garlic and pork crackling.

Coquito — A creamy coconut-and-rum liqueur, typical at Christmas time.
Medalla Light — The local, light island beer.

paragraphs: Hire a car for day trips, as public transport is barely developed outside San Juan.

h1: Travel Spanish for Colombia
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Colombia?
intro: Colombian Spanish, especially from Bogotá, is considered very clear and easy to understand. Notable is the widespread polite 'usted', even among friends, and 'su merced' in the Andean highlands. On the Caribbean coast it's spoken faster and the 's' is often swallowed. sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Colombian Spanish, especially from Bogotá, is considered very clear and easy to understand. Notable is the widespread polite 'usted', even among friends, and 'su merced' in the Andean highlands. On the Caribbean coast it's spoken faster and the 's' is often swallowed. Indigenous languages such as Wayuunaiki exist, but Spanish dominates everyday life.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Colombia stretches from Caribbean beaches across the three branches of the Andes all the way to the Amazon. Backpacker highlights include the colonial city of Cartagena, the coffee triangle around Salento, Medellín and the Caribbean coast around Tayrona. The country offers enormous scenic and cultural variety.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, peoples such as the Muisca lived here, famous for their wealth of gold (the El Dorado legend). From the 16th century the area was colonised by Spain, and in 1819 Simón Bolívar won independence as part of Gran Colombia. The 20th century was marked by civil war, guerrillas (the FARC) and drug cartels. Since the 2016 peace agreement, the security situation has improved markedly in many regions.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 53 million inhabitants (2025) – the second most populous country in South America.
A presidential republic; in 2022 the country elected Gustavo Petro as its first left-wing president.
The third-largest economy in South America, recovering after the peace process with the FARC.
Oil and coal, coffee, flowers, mining (gold, emeralds) and growing tourism.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Bandeja paisa — A lavish plate of beans, rice, mince, chicharrón, egg, plantain and avocado.
Arepa — Grilled or fried corn flatbreads, often filled with cheese or egg.
Ajiaco — A hearty chicken soup from Bogotá with three kinds of potato, corn and guascas herb.
Empanadas — Fried corn pasties, filled with meat and potato.
Aguardiente — An anise-forward cane-sugar spirit, the Colombian national drink.
Tinto — A small, often sweet black coffee, sold everywhere at street stalls.
Lulada — A refreshing drink from the tart lulo fruit, popular in Cali.

h1: Travel Spanish for Venezuela
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Venezuela?
intro: Venezuelan Spanish sounds Caribbean: fast, melodic and with the 's' at the end of a syllable often swallowed. Diminutives ending in '-ico' rather than '-ito' are common (e.g. 'ahorita' becomes 'ahoritica'). The country is famous for its easy-going slang and nicknames for just about everyone…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Venezuelan Spanish sounds Caribbean: fast, melodic and with the 's' at the end of a syllable often swallowed. Diminutives ending in '-ico' rather than '-ito' are common (e.g. 'ahorita' becomes 'ahoritica'). The country is famous for its easy-going slang and nicknames for just about everyone. Indigenous languages such as Wayuunaiki and Warao are still spoken in some regions.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Venezuela sits on the Caribbean coast and ranges from beaches across the Andes to the Amazon and the Gran Sabana with its table mountains (tepuis). This is where Angel Falls, the highest waterfall on Earth, plunges into the depths. Because of the political and economic crisis, travel is currently difficult and takes careful preparation.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, indigenous peoples such as the Carib and Arawak lived here. Spain colonised the area from the 16th century; in 1811 the struggle for independence began under Simón Bolívar, who was born here. In the 20th century, oil made Venezuela rich for a time. Since the 2000s, mismanagement and hyperinflation have led to a severe crisis and mass emigration.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 28 million inhabitants (2025); over 7 million have emigrated since 2015.
A presidential republic, internationally disputed and ruled authoritatively under Nicolás Maduro.
A deep, long-running economic crisis with hyperinflation despite the world's largest oil reserves.
Oil (over 90% of exports), increasingly mining (gold) and remittances from abroad.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Arepa — A stuffed corn flatbread, the Venezuelan staple par excellence.
Pabellón criollo — A national dish of braised shredded beef, black beans, rice and plantain.
Cachapa — A sweetish pancake made from fresh corn, often filled with cheese.
Hallaca — A Christmas dish of corn dough with a braised filling, cooked in banana leaves.
Ron venezolano — A high-quality rum, prized worldwide for its quality.
Papelón con limón — A refresher of cane sugar (papelón) and lime.
Chicha — A sweet, silky rice-based drink with cinnamon.

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „chamo/chama" — bloke / girl, mate
„pana" — mate, good friend
„chévere" — cool, great
„vaina" — thing, stuff (all-purpose word)
„arrecho" — annoyed/angry or wild (depending on context)
„burda" — very, totally (intensifier)

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: Read up thoroughly on the current security and supply situation before you travel, and bring plenty of cash (US dollars), as cards often don't work.

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: The Andean Spanish of Ecuador is considered slow and clear, ideal for learning Spanish. Quechua (locally 'Kichwa') is a widely spoken indigenous language and has contributed many words. The diminutive suffix '-ito/-ita' is often used politely, even on words like 'ahorita' or 'despacito'. On the coast it's spoken faster and more Caribbean than in the highlands.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Ecuador is one of the smallest yet most diverse countries in South America: coast, volcano-studded Andes, Amazon and the unique Galápagos Islands. Backpackers love Quito's old town, the volcano road ('Avenida de los Volcanes') and Baños as an adventure hub. Thanks to its compact size, the regions are quick to reach.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before the Spanish conquest, the Andean highlands were part of the Inca Empire, with Quito as an important centre. Spain colonised the area from the 16th century; in 1822, after the Battle of Pichincha, it gained independence and was initially part of Gran Colombia. The independent state of Ecuador came into being in 1830. Since 2000 the US dollar has been the official currency.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 18 million inhabitants (2025).
A presidential republic; it has used the US dollar as its currency since 2000.
A dollarised economy; recently strained by a sharp rise in drug-related crime.
Oil, bananas (the world's largest exporter), shrimp, flowers and cocoa.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Ceviche — Ecuadorean ceviche, often with prawns and in a tomatoey marinade.
Encebollado — A hearty fish soup with tuna, cassava and onions, regarded as a hangover breakfast.
Llapingachos — Fried potato-and-cheese cakes, usually served with peanut sauce.
Hornado — Slow-roasted, crispy pork from the highlands.
Canelazo — A hot drink of cane spirit, cinnamon and naranjilla, perfect against the Andean chill.
Pilsener — Ecuador's most popular home-grown lager.
Colada morada — A thick, spiced berry drink, traditionally for All Souls' Day.

„¿Tiene sencillo? Solo tengo un billete de veinte." — Do you have change? I only have a twenty.
„Voy a comprar algo en la tienda." — I'll quickly buy something at the corner shop.

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: Make the most of the country's compact size: by bus you can reach the coast, the Andes and the jungle from Quito in just a few hours each.

h1: Travel Spanish for Peru
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Peru?
intro: Peruvian Andean Spanish is usually clear and easy to understand. Quechua is a second official language and a part of everyday life, especially around Cusco; Aymara is also spoken by Lake Titicaca. Many everyday words come from Quechua, such as 'wawa' (baby) or 'chacra' (field). On the coast and in…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Peruvian Andean Spanish is usually clear and easy to understand. Quechua is a second official language and a part of everyday life, especially around Cusco; Aymara is also spoken by Lake Titicaca. Many everyday words come from Quechua, such as 'wawa' (baby) or 'chacra' (field). On the coast and in Lima it's spoken faster than in the highlands.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Peru brings together desert coastline, high Andes and Amazon rainforest. The absolute highlight is Machu Picchu, along with Cusco, the Sacred Valley, Lake Titicaca and the Colca Canyon. Lima is also regarded as the culinary capital of Latin America.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Peru was the heartland of the Inca Empire, with Cusco as its capital, preceded by earlier civilisations such as the Nazca, Moche and Chimú. In 1532 Francisco Pizarro conquered the empire for Spain, which then established the powerful Viceroyalty of Peru. In 1821 José de San Martín declared independence. Today Peru is a centre of Andean culture, where the Quechua tradition remains alive to this day.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 34.5 million inhabitants (2025).
A presidential republic with ongoing political instability and frequent changes of president.
A commodity-dependent economy with long growth but high informality.
Mining (copper, gold, silver – among the world's leaders), fishmeal, agriculture and tourism (Machu Picchu).

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Ceviche — Raw fish marinated in lime juice with onions, chilli and sweet potato, the national dish.
Lomo saltado — Stir-fried beef with onions, tomatoes and chips, with a Chinese influence.
Ají de gallina — A creamy chicken stew with yellow chilli (ají amarillo).
Causa limeña — A layered, cold potato terrine with lime and a savoury filling.
Pisco Sour — A cocktail of pisco, lime, egg white and sugar syrup, the national cocktail.
Inca Kola — A bright-yellow, very sweet cult soft drink with a bubblegum flavour.
Chicha morada — A sweet, non-alcoholic drink of purple corn with spices.

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „pata" — mate, friend
„chévere" — cool, great
„bacán" — brilliant, great
„causa" — mate (also a potato dish)
„jato" — house, place
„al toque" — right away, in a flash

sections.heading: Example sentences from the app
bullets: „¿Dónde tomo un taxi del aeropuerto?" — Where do I get a taxi from the airport?
„Lléveme a Miraflores, por favor." — Take me to Miraflores, please.
„¿Cuánto cobra hasta el centro?" — How much is the ride to the centre?
„Quiero caminar por el malecón." — I'd like to walk along the seafront malecón.
„¿Es seguro caminar por esta zona?" — Is this area safe to walk in?
„Me siento mal por la altura." — I feel sick from the altitude.
„¿Tiene mate de coca para el soroche?" — Do you have coca tea for the altitude?
„¿Hay una farmacia por aquí?" — Is there a pharmacy near here?
„Necesito agua, por favor." — I need water, please.
„Hoy necesito aclimatarme a la altura." — Today I need to acclimatise to the altitude.
„¿A qué hora sale el bus cama a Arequipa?" — When does the night bus to Arequipa leave?
„Tengo un boleto de tren a Aguas Calientes." — I have a train ticket to Aguas Calientes.
„¿Hay colectivo al Valle Sagrado?" — Is there a shared taxi to the Sacred Valley?
„¿Cuánto dura el viaje?" — How long is the journey?

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: In Cusco (3,400 m), allow a day or two to acclimatise to the altitude and drink mate de coca to fend off altitude sickness.

h1: Travel Spanish for Bolivia
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Bolivia?
intro: People here speak an Andean Spanish that sounds relatively clear and slow and is easy for learners to follow. Alongside Spanish, Quechua and Aymara are official languages and widely spoken in the highlands. Many everyday words come from Quechua/Aymara, such as 'wawa' (baby/child). Voseo is barely…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: People here speak an Andean Spanish that sounds relatively clear and slow and is easy for learners to follow. Alongside Spanish, Quechua and Aymara are official languages and widely spoken in the highlands. Many everyday words come from Quechua/Aymara, such as 'wawa' (baby/child). Voseo is barely used here; people mostly use 'tú'.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Bolivia is a landlocked country in the heart of the Andes at staggering altitude: La Paz sits at around 3,600 m, and El Alto higher still. Backpacker highlights include the Salar de Uyuni, Lake Titicaca and the Death Road near La Paz. It's one of the cheapest destinations in South America.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, the highlands were part of the Inca Empire, and before that the Tiwanaku culture flourished by Lake Titicaca. From the 16th century the Spanish exploited the silver mines of Potosí, which underpinned colonial wealth. In 1825 Bolivia became independent and was named after Simón Bolívar. Today the country has one of the highest proportions of indigenous people in South America and sees itself as a plurinational state.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 12.4 million inhabitants (2025).
A plurinational presidential republic with two capitals (Sucre and La Paz); in 2025 nearly 20 years of socialist MAS government came to an end.
One of the poorest economies in South America, heavily dependent on commodities.
Natural gas, mining (tin, silver, lithium), agriculture (soya, quinoa) and coca.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Salteña — A juicy stuffed pasty with meat, egg and broth, the typical mid-morning snack.
Pique a lo macho — A hearty plate of beef, sausages, chips, onions and a spicy sauce.
Silpancho — Thinly pounded breaded meat on rice and potatoes, with a fried egg.
Sopa de maní — A creamy peanut soup with meat and potatoes, a classic.
Singani — A Bolivian grape brandy, the national spirit, often served as a 'Chuflay' with ginger ale.
Chicha — A fermented corn drink with a long indigenous tradition, mildly alcoholic.
Mate de coca — A tea made from coca leaves, helps with altitude sickness (soroche).

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „wawa" — baby/toddler (from Quechua/Aymara)
„chango/a" — boy/girl, young person
„¡qué macana!" — how annoying! / what a pain!
„cholita" — indigenous woman in traditional dress
„yapa" — a free extra thrown in when you buy something
„ch'aki" — hangover (from Quechua)

sections.heading: Example sentences from the app
bullets: „Hola, ¿dónde tomo un taxi del aeropuerto de El Alto?" — Hi, where do I find a taxi from El Alto airport?
„Lléveme al centro de La Paz, por favor." — Take me to the centre of La Paz, please.
„¿Cuánto me cobra hasta el hostal?" — How much do you charge to the hostel?
„Me afecta la altura. ¿Tiene mate de coca?" — I feel the altitude. Do you have coca tea?
„Tengo mareo por la altura." — I feel dizzy because of the altitude.
„Buenas tardes, ¿cómo está?" — Good afternoon, how are you?
„¡Kamisaraki!" — Hello! (greeting in Aymara)
„Waliki, gracias." — I'm fine, thanks. (reply in Aymara)
„¿Dónde puedo sacar bolivianos?" — Where can I withdraw bolivianos?
„¿Me puede cambiar dinero?" — Can you change money for me?
„¿Cómo llego a El Alto en el teleférico?" — How do I get to El Alto by cable car?
„¿Qué línea del teleférico va al centro?" — Which cable-car line goes to the centre?
„¿Este minibús va al Mercado de las Brujas?" — Does this minibus go to the Witches' Market?
„¿Dónde para el trufi a San Pedro?" — Where does the trufi to San Pedro stop?

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: When you arrive in La Paz or Uyuni, allow a quiet day or two to acclimatise to the altitude and drink coca tea to fend off altitude sickness.

faq.question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Bolivia?

h1: Travel Spanish for Chile
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Chile?
intro: Chilean Spanish is considered one of the hardest in Latin America: very fast, with swallowed final syllables and lots of slang (chilenismos). Endings in '-ado' often become '-ao' (e.g. 'cansao' instead of 'cansado'). A distinctive voseo verb form is common ('¿cachái?' instead of '¿cachas?'). In…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Chilean Spanish is considered one of the hardest in Latin America: very fast, with swallowed final syllables and lots of slang (chilenismos). Endings in '-ado' often become '-ao' (e.g. 'cansao' instead of 'cansado'). A distinctive voseo verb form is common ('¿cachái?' instead of '¿cachas?'). In the south there are Mapudungun influences, and many place names come from Mapuche.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Chile is an extremely long, narrow country stretching over more than 4,000 km along the Pacific. It ranges from the Atacama Desert in the north through the central wine country to the fjords and mountains of Patagonia. Highlights for backpackers are the starry skies of the Atacama, Torres del Paine National Park and Easter Island.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, the Mapuche lived in the south, long resisting the Spanish conquest successfully. From 1541 the Spanish founded Santiago, and in 1818 Chile gained its independence. The 20th century was marked by the overthrow of Salvador Allende in 1973 and the military dictatorship under Pinochet until 1990. Today Chile is considered one of the most economically stable countries in the region.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 19.6 million inhabitants (2025).
A stable presidential democracy with peaceful transfers of power; in 2022 it elected Gabriel Boric, one of the world's youngest presidents.
One of the wealthiest and most open economies in Latin America.
Copper (the world's largest producer) and lithium, wine, fruit, salmon and forestry.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Empanada de pino — A baked pasty filled with mince, onion, egg and olive.
Completo — A Chilean hot dog piled high with avocado, tomato and mayonnaise.
Pastel de choclo — A corn-purée bake over a meat filling, popular in summer.
Cazuela — A hearty stew with meat, squash, corn and potato.
Pisco sour — A cocktail of pisco, lemon and sugar, a popular aperitif (Peru claims it too).
Terremoto — A sweet mix of white wine, pineapple ice cream and grenadine that packs a punch.
Vino chileno — Chilean wine, especially bold Carménère and Cabernet, world-renowned.

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „cachái" — get it? / understand?
„weón/huevón" — mate/bloke (used among friends, depending on tone)
„bacán" — great, cool, brilliant
„po" — filler word at the end of a sentence (from 'pues')
„la raja" — great, top-notch (colloquial)
„fome" — boring, dull

sections.heading: Example sentences from the app
bullets: „¿Dónde encuentro un taxi al centro de Santiago?" — Where can I find a taxi to downtown Santiago?

„¿Cuánto cuesta el viaje desde el aeropuerto?" — How much is the ride from the airport?

„¿Todo bien? ¿Cachái?" — All good with you? Get it? (slang)
„¡Qué bacán!" — That's really cool!
„Hagámoslo al tiro, ¿ya?" — Let's do it right away, yeah?
„¡Sí po, weón!" — Yeah sure, man! (colloquial)
„Disculpe, ¿dónde está la estación de metro?" — Excuse me, where is the metro station?
„¿Qué línea va a la Plaza de Armas?" — Which line goes to Plaza de Armas?
„¿Esta micro va al Cerro San Cristóbal?" — Does this bus go to Cerro San Cristóbal?
„¿Cuánto cuesta un colectivo hasta allá?" — How much is a shared taxi there?
„Voy a tomar un bus a Valparaíso." — I'll take a long-distance bus to Valparaíso.
„¿Qué ascensor me sube al cerro?" — Which funicular takes me up the hill?

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: Chile is considerably more expensive than its neighbours; cashless payment is widespread, so budget accordingly higher.

h1: Travel Spanish for Argentina
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Argentina?
intro: Argentine Spanish (Rioplatense) is instantly recognisable by its voseo: 'vos' replaces 'tú' ('vos tenés' instead of 'tú tienes'). Notable is the pronunciation of 'll' and 'y' like an English 'sh' ('calle' sounds like 'cashe'). Italian influence gives the speech an almost sing-song melody. In the…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Argentine Spanish (Rioplatense) is instantly recognisable by its voseo: 'vos' replaces 'tú' ('vos tenés' instead of 'tú tienes'). Notable is the pronunciation of 'll' and 'y' like an English 'sh' ('calle' sounds like 'cashe'). Italian influence gives the speech an almost sing-song melody. In the north-west and north-east there are Quechua and Guaraní influences respectively.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Argentina is the second-largest country in South America, stretching from the subtropical Iguazú Falls to windswept Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego in the south. Buenos Aires is one of the liveliest cities on the continent. Highlights include the Perito Moreno glacier, the Mendoza wine region and the colourful Andes of the north-west.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, Andean peoples lived in the north and nomadic groups on the Pampas and in Patagonia. The Spanish established settlements from the 16th century, and in 1816 Argentina declared its independence. In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, millions of European immigrants arrived, above all from Italy and Spain. Recent history has been shaped by Peronism, the military dictatorship (1976–1983) and recurring economic crises.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 46 million inhabitants (2025).
A federal presidential republic; since late 2023 headed by libertarian president Javier Milei.
The third-largest economy in the region, but chronically plagued by high inflation and debt.
Agricultural exports (soya, beef, maize, wine), energy (Vaca Muerta shale) and industry.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Asado — A traditional barbecue of various cuts of beef, a social ritual.
Empanadas — Stuffed pasties, filled with meat, cheese or corn depending on the region.
Milanesa — A breaded escalope, often done 'napolitana' topped with ham and cheese.
Choripán — A grilled chorizo sausage in bread with chimichurri, a classic street food.
Mate — An infusion of yerba mate leaves, drunk from a gourd with a bombilla, a social ritual.
Vino Malbec — Argentina's characterful red wine variety, above all from Mendoza.
Fernet con Coca — A bitter liqueur mixed with cola, the unofficial national drink of the young.

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „che" — hey/mate (typical Argentine way of addressing someone)
„boludo/a" — idiot, but among friends also 'mate'
„laburo" — work, job (from Italian)
„quilombo" — chaos, mess
„copado" — cool, brilliant
„¿qué onda?" — What's up? / How's it going?

bullets: „Hola, ¿dónde tomo un taxi en el aeropuerto?" — Hi, where do I get a taxi from the airport?

„Che, ¿todo bien? ¿De dónde sos?" — Hey, all good? Where are you from?
„¿Querés venir esta noche?" — Do you want to come along tonight?

„¿Dónde puedo cargar la tarjeta SUBE?" — Where can I top up the SUBE card?
„¿Qué colectivo va al centro?" — Which bus goes to the centre?
„Disculpá, ¿el subte va a Recoleta?" — Excuse me, does the metro go to Recoleta?
„Una mesa para dos, por favor." — A table for two, please.
„Quiero un bife de chorizo, a punto." — I'd like a bife de chorizo, medium.
„¿Qué me recomendás del asado?" — What do you recommend from the asado?
„Tres empanadas para llevar, por favor." — Three empanadas to go, please.
„Voy a pedir una milanesa con papas fritas." — I'll have a milanesa with fries.
„Dame un choripán, por favor." — Give me a choripán, please.

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: Because of inflation, the unofficial 'dólar blue' exchange rate is often worth it; bring US dollars in cash and check the current rates once you're there.

h1: Travel Spanish for Uruguay
question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Uruguay?
intro: Uruguay shares Rioplatense Spanish with Argentina: voseo ('vos') and the 'sh' pronunciation of 'll' and 'y' are standard. On the border with Brazil, a mixed dialect of Spanish and Portuguese is spoken ('Portuñol'). The tone is similar to that of Buenos Aires, but is considered a bit calmer…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Uruguay shares Rioplatense Spanish with Argentina: voseo ('vos') and the 'sh' pronunciation of 'll' and 'y' are standard. On the border with Brazil, a mixed dialect of Spanish and Portuguese is spoken ('Portuñol'). The tone is similar to that of Buenos Aires, but is considered a bit calmer. Indigenous languages are barely present today.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Uruguay is the smallest Spanish-speaking country in South America, lying between Argentina and Brazil on the Río de la Plata. It's known for its laid-back atmosphere, long Atlantic beaches and a strong mate culture. Highlights are the old town of Colonia del Sacramento, the beaches of Punta del Este and easy-going Montevideo.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, the indigenous Charrúa people lived here. The Spanish and Portuguese long fought over the territory until, in 1828, Uruguay became independent as a buffer state between Argentina and Brazil. In the 20th century, thanks to its welfare state, it was known as the 'Switzerland of South America'. Today Uruguay is one of the most stable and liberal countries in the region.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: About 3.4 million inhabitants (2025).
A stable presidential democracy; regarded as one of the least corrupt countries in the region.
High per-capita income, a strong middle class and a solid welfare state.
Agricultural exports (beef, soya, dairy, wool), forestry, software and tourism.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Chivito — A lavish sandwich with steak, ham, cheese, egg and salad, the national dish.
Asado — A beef barbecue, in Uruguay every bit as important as in Argentina.
Milanesa — A breaded escalope, popular as a sandwich ('milanesa al pan').
Choripán — A grilled sausage in bread, the classic street food at a barbecue.
Mate — The national drink bar none; Uruguayans carry a flask and a mate gourd everywhere.
Medio y medio — A refreshing mix of white wine and sparkling wine, a classic from Montevideo.
Grappamiel — A grappa brandy with honey, warming in winter.

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „ta" — okay, fine (short for 'está')
„bo" — hey/mate (Uruguayan form of address, like 'che')
„championes" — trainers, sneakers
„de más" — great, fantastic
„garra charrúa" — fighting spirit, perseverance (a point of national pride)

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: Uruguayans drink mate all the time and everywhere; do accept an invitation to share it, but don't stir the bombilla (the metal straw), as that's considered rude.

faq.question: What kind of Spanish is spoken in Uruguay?
faq.answer: Uruguay shares Rioplatense Spanish with Argentina: voseo ('vos') and the 'sh' pronunciation of 'll' and 'y' are standard. The HolaRuta app drills exactly this accent and vocabulary before you land.

faq.answer: Common examples include: „ta" (okay, fine (short for 'está')), „bo" (hey/mate (Uruguayan form of address, like 'che')), „championes" (trainers, sneakers), „de más" (great, fantastic). HolaRuta turns these into flashcards so you have them ready on the road.

faq.answer: In local Spanish: "bo". You can practise this exact phrase as a flashcard in the HolaRuta app, pronunciation tip included.

In everyday life many mix the two into 'Jopara', a fluid switching between Spanish and Guaraní. Paraguayan Spanish uses voseo too. Learn a few words of Guaraní and…

sections.heading: What Spanish is spoken here?
paragraphs: Paraguay is officially bilingual: alongside Spanish, the vast majority speak Guaraní, an indigenous language they take real pride in. In everyday life many mix the two into 'Jopara', a fluid switching between Spanish and Guaraní. Paraguayan Spanish uses voseo too. Learn a few words of Guaraní and you'll quickly win people over.

sections.heading: Country & culture
paragraphs: Paraguay is a little-visited landlocked country in the heart of South America, split by the Río Paraguay into the humid east and the dry Chaco in the west. It's authentic, cheap and barely developed for tourism. Highlights include the Jesuit mission ruins, the vast Chaco wilderness and the laid-back capital Asunción.

sections.heading: History in brief
paragraphs: Before colonial times, the area was settled by Guaraní peoples. In 1537 the Spanish founded Asunción, one of the oldest cities on the continent; the Jesuit reductions became famous. Paraguay became independent in 1811. The devastating War of the Triple Alliance (1864–1870) against Brazil, Argentina and Uruguay cost the lives of a large part of the male population.

sections.heading: Country & people
bullets: Around 6.9 million inhabitants (2025).
A presidential republic, long dominated by the Colorado Party.
A growing, agriculture-based economy with low taxes and cheap energy.
Soya and beef exports, huge hydropower (Itaipú) and agriculture.

sections.heading: Food & drink
bullets: Sopa paraguaya — Despite the name not a soup, but a hearty corn-bread cake with cheese.
Chipa — A ring-shaped bake of cassava flour and cheese, sold everywhere as a snack.
Mbejú — A flatbread of cassava starch and cheese, cooked crisp in a pan.
Bori bori — A hearty soup with corn-and-cheese dumplings, often with chicken.
Tereré — Ice-cold infused mate with herbs, the national drink against the heat.
Mosto — Freshly pressed sugar-cane juice, sweet and refreshing.
Caña — A cane-sugar spirit, the most common local distillate.

sections.heading: Useful words & slang
bullets: „mba'éichapa" — How are you? (Guaraní greeting)
„argel" — unpleasant, annoying (Paraguayan slang)
„nde" — hey/mate (from Guaraní)
„tranquilopa" — totally relaxed (a mix of Spanish and Guaraní)
„purete" — great, cool, brilliant
„luego" — an intensifying filler word at the end of a sentence (with its own meaning in Paraguay)

sections.heading: Travel tip
paragraphs: Drink ice-cold tereré like the locals to beat the heat and learn a few words of Guaraní; in Paraguay that opens plenty of doors and hearts.

sections.heading: Für wen ist HolaRuta?
paragraphs: HolaRuta richtet sich an Backpacker und Reisende, die schnell die Spanisch-Sätze brauchen, die vor Ort wirklich vorkommen - nicht an Menschen, die eine komplette Sprache von Grund auf lernen wollen.

bullets: Situativ statt kursförmig: Karten sind nach echten Reise-Momenten sortiert (Bus, Hostel, Grenze, Notfall), nicht nach Grammatik-Lektionen.

h1: HolaRuta vs. Duolingo & Babbel: Was ist der Unterschied?
question: HolaRuta vs. Duolingo & Babbel: Was ist der Unterschied?
intro: HolaRuta tritt nicht als bessere Allzweck-Sprachschule gegen Duolingo oder Babbel an, sondern besetzt eine Nische, die beide bewusst nicht bedienen: situatives, regional korrektes Reise-Spanisch für Lateinamerika, offline und ohne Werbung.

sections.heading: Duolingo: Stärke und Lücke
paragraphs: Duolingo punktet mit Gamification, riesiger Reichweite und einer kostenlosen Basisversion. Die Lücke: generisches "Schul-Spanisch" (oft kastilisches/neutrales Spanisch), gamifiziert statt situativ, mit viel Tracking und Werbung - konkrete Reise-Notfall-Situationen kommen kaum vor.

sections.heading: Babbel: Stärke und Lücke
paragraphs: Babbel bietet strukturierte Kurse und gute Dialoge. Die Lücke: Abo-Pflicht, kursförmiger Aufbau statt "schnell die richtige Karte am Busbahnhof", und lateinamerikanische Varianten nur teilweise abgedeckt.

sections.heading: Wo HolaRuta gewinnt
bullets: LatAm-korrekt statt kastilisches Lehrbuch-Spanisch (colectivo, vuelto, plata, chévere, celular).
Situativ statt kursförmig: kuratiert nach echten Reise-Momenten (Taxi, Hostel, Grenze, Notfall, Smalltalk).
Offline & PWA-leicht, installierbar ohne App Store.
Keine Werbung, kein Konto-Zwang, Fortschritt bleibt lokal.

faq.question: Ist HolaRuta besser als Duolingo?
faq.answer: HolaRuta und Duolingo lösen unterschiedliche Probleme: Duolingo eignet sich für langfristigen, gamifizierten Sprachaufbau; HolaRuta ist auf schnelles, situatives Reise-Spanisch für Lateinamerika spezialisiert - inklusive Wörtern, die Duolingo oft gar nicht abdeckt.

faq.question: Was kostet HolaRuta im Vergleich zu Babbel?
faq.answer: HolaRuta ist komplett kostenlos und ohne Abo, während Babbel ein kostenpflichtiges Abonnement voraussetzt.

faq.question: Deckt Duolingo lateinamerikanisches Spanisch ab?

h1: Lateinamerikanisches Spanisch vs. Spanien-Spanisch: die echten Unterschiede
question: Lateinamerikanisches Spanisch vs. Spanien-Spanisch: die echten Unterschiede
intro: Lateinamerikanisches Spanisch unterscheidet sich in drei großen Punkten vom Spanisch Spaniens: Aussprache (Seseo statt des kastilischen "th"-Lauts), Grammatik (Voseo - "vos" statt "tú" in vielen Ländern) und Alltagswortschatz (celular, plata, chévere statt móvil, dinero, guay).

sections.heading: Aussprache: Seseo
paragraphs: In Spanien werden "c" (vor e/i) und "z" wie das englische "th" in "think" ausgesprochen (Distinción). Fast ganz Lateinamerika nutzt stattdessen Seseo: Diese Buchstaben klingen wie "s" - "cielo" und "sierra" reimen sich.

sections.heading: Grammatik: Voseo vs. Tú
paragraphs: Spanien nutzt "tú" für die informelle Anrede. Viele lateinamerikanische Länder (besonders Argentinien, Uruguay und Teile Zentralamerikas) nutzen stattdessen "vos" mit eigenen Verbformen ("vos tenés" statt "tú tienes"). Andere Länder wie Mexiko nutzen "tú" genau wie Spanien.

sections.heading: Wortschatz: Alltagswörter unterscheiden sich
bullets: "celular" (Lateinamerika) vs. "móvil" (Spanien) - Handy
"plata" (Lateinamerika) vs. "dinero"/"pasta" (Spanien) - Geld
"chévere" (viele LatAm-Länder) vs. "guay" (Spanien) - cool/super
"carro"/"auto" (Lateinamerika) vs. "coche" (Spanien) - Auto

sections.heading: Länder-Beispiele
bullets: Argentinien (Rioplatense-Voseo): Das argentinische Spanisch (Rioplatense) ist sofort am Voseo erkennbar: 'vos' ersetzt 'tú' ('vos tenés' statt 'tú tienes'). Auffällig ist die Aussprache von…
Mexiko (kein Voseo, nutzt tú): Das mexikanische Spanisch gilt als klar und gut verständlich, mit weichem Tonfall. Voseo gibt es nicht – man verwendet 'tú'. Typisch sind viele…
Kolumbien (verbreitetes höfliches usted): Kolumbianisches Spanisch, besonders aus Bogotá, gilt als sehr klar und gut verständlich. Auffällig ist das verbreitete höfliche 'usted' auch unter Freunden…

faq.question: Ist lateinamerikanisches Spanisch in jedem Land gleich?
faq.answer: Nein. Jedes Land hat seinen eigenen Akzent, Slang und teilweise eigene Grammatik (wie das Voseo). Die Länder-Guides von HolaRuta zeigen, was für jedes Reiseziel spezifisch ist.

faq.question: Wird Spanien-Spanisch in Lateinamerika verstanden?
faq.answer: Ja, Spanien-Spanisch wird in ganz Lateinamerika verstanden - aber Klang und Wortschatz markieren dich klar als Ausländer, und manche Wörter unterscheiden sich genug, um Verwirrung zu stiften (z. B. ist "coger" in Spanien neutral, in mehreren lateinamerikanischen Ländern aber vulgär). HolaRuta lehrt gezielt die lateinamerikanischen Varianten, damit dir genau das nicht passiert.

faq.question: Lehrt HolaRuta Spanien-Spanisch oder lateinamerikanisches Spanisch?
faq.answer: HolaRuta lehrt durchgängig lateinamerika-korrektes Spanisch - die Wörter und Sätze, die Reisende vor Ort wirklich hören, nicht kastilisches Lehrbuch-Spanisch.

h1: Wie funktioniert Spaced Repetition in HolaRuta?
question: Wie funktioniert Spaced Repetition in HolaRuta?
intro: HolaRuta nutzt einen vereinfachten SM-2-Algorithmus: Nach jeder Bewertung berechnet die App neu, wann eine Karte am effizientesten wieder auftauchen sollte - Karten, die du gut kannst, werden seltener wiederholt; Karten, die du vergisst, kommen schneller zurück.

sections.heading: So läuft eine Wiederholung in HolaRuta ab
bullets: HolaRuta zeigt dir eine fällige Karteikarte (Frage auf Deutsch, Antwort auf Spanisch).
Du bewertest ehrlich: "Nochmal" (falsch/vergessen), "Gut" (richtig, aber Mühe) oder "Leicht" (sofort gewusst).
Der HolaRuta-Algorithmus passt das Intervall bis zur nächsten Wiederholung an: "Nochmal" setzt es zurück, "Gut"/"Leicht" verlängern es.
Die Karte taucht automatisch am berechneten Tag wieder auf - nie zu früh, nie zu spät.

sections.heading: Was steckt technisch dahinter?
paragraphs: In HolaRuta trägt jede Karte einen Lernzustand aus Ease-Faktor, Intervall (in Tagen), Fälligkeitsdatum und Wiederholungszähler. Der Ease-Faktor ist auf einen sinnvollen Bereich begrenzt, damit Karten nie endlos leicht oder endlos schwer werden.

faq.question: Was ist Spaced Repetition?
faq.answer: Spaced Repetition ist eine Lerntechnik, bei der Wiederholungen in wachsenden Abständen stattfinden, statt stumpf immer wieder alles zu wiederholen. HolaRuta setzt das mit einem vereinfachten SM-2-Algorithmus um.

faq.question: Warum vergesse ich Vokabeln, wenn ich nicht mit Spaced Repetition lerne?
faq.answer: Ohne geplante Wiederholung verblasst neu Gelerntes schnell (die "Vergessenskurve"). HolaRuta wiederholt eine Karte genau dann, wenn du kurz davor bist, sie zu vergessen - das festigt sie langfristig, statt sie verpuffen zu lassen.

faq.question: Muss ich in HolaRuta jeden Tag lernen?
faq.answer: Nein, aber regelmäßiges, kurzes Lernen funktioniert mit Spaced Repetition am besten - HolaRuta zeigt dir jeden Tag nur die Karten, die gerade wirklich fällig sind.

h1: HolaRuta vs. Duolingo & Babbel: what's the difference?
question: HolaRuta vs. Duolingo & Babbel: what's the difference?
intro: HolaRuta doesn't try to out-compete Duolingo or Babbel as a better general-purpose language school - it occupies a niche both deliberately don't serve: situational, regionally accurate travel Spanish for Latin America, offline and ad-free.

sections.heading: Duolingo: strength and gap
paragraphs: Duolingo wins on gamification, huge reach and a free tier. The gap: generic, often Castilian/neutral Spanish, gamified rather than situational, with heavy tracking and ads - concrete travel-emergency situations barely appear.

sections.heading: Babbel: strength and gap
paragraphs: Babbel offers structured courses and solid dialogues. The gap: a subscription requirement, a course-shaped structure rather than "quickly find the right card at the bus terminal", and only partial coverage of Latin American variants.

sections.heading: Where HolaRuta wins
bullets: Latin-America-correct instead of textbook Castilian Spanish (colectivo, vuelto, plata, chévere, celular).
Situational, not course-shaped: curated around real travel moments (taxi, hostel, border, emergency, small talk).
Offline & PWA-light, installable without an app store.
No ads, no account required, progress stays local.

faq.question: Is HolaRuta better than Duolingo?
faq.answer: HolaRuta and Duolingo solve different problems: Duolingo suits long-term, gamified language building; HolaRuta specialises in fast, situational travel Spanish for Latin America - including words Duolingo often doesn't cover at all.

faq.question: How much does HolaRuta cost compared to Babbel?
faq.answer: HolaRuta is completely free with no subscription, while Babbel requires a paid subscription.

faq.question: Does Duolingo cover Latin American Spanish?
faq.answer: Duolingo mostly uses generic or Castilian Spanish. HolaRuta is built throughout around regionally accurate Latin American Spanish.

h1: Latin American Spanish vs. Spain Spanish: the real differences
question: Latin American Spanish vs. Spain Spanish: the real differences
intro: Latin American Spanish differs from Spain's in three big ways: pronunciation (seseo instead of the Castilian "th" sound), grammar (voseo - "vos" instead of "tú" in many countries), and everyday vocabulary (celular, plata, chévere instead of móvil, dinero, guay).

sections.heading: Pronunciation: seseo
paragraphs: In Spain, "c" (before e/i) and "z" are pronounced like the English "th" in "think" (distinción). Almost all of Latin America uses seseo instead: these letters are pronounced like "s" - "cielo" and "sierra" rhyme.

sections.heading: Grammar: voseo vs. tú
paragraphs: Spain uses "tú" for informal "you". Many Latin American countries (especially Argentina, Uruguay, and parts of Central America) use "vos" instead, with its own verb forms ("vos tenés" instead of "tú tienes"). Other countries, like Mexico, use "tú" just like Spain.

sections.heading: Vocabulary: everyday words differ
bullets: "celular" (Latin America) vs. "móvil" (Spain) - mobile phone
"plata" (Latin America) vs. "dinero"/"pasta" (Spain) - money
"chévere" (many LatAm countries) vs. "guay" (Spain) - cool/great
"carro"/"auto" (Latin America) vs. "coche" (Spain) - car

sections.heading: Country examples
bullets: Argentina (Rioplatense voseo): Argentine Spanish (Rioplatense) is instantly recognisable by its voseo: 'vos' replaces 'tú' ('vos tenés' instead of 'tú tienes'). Notable is the pronunciation…
Mexico (no voseo, uses tú): Mexican Spanish is considered clear and easy to understand, with a gentle lilt. There's no voseo – people use 'tú'. It's full of Nahuatl loanwords like…
Colombia (widespread polite usted): Colombian Spanish, especially from Bogotá, is considered very clear and easy to understand. Notable is the widespread polite 'usted', even among friends, and…

faq.question: Is Latin American Spanish the same in every country?
faq.answer: No. Each country has its own accent, slang and, in some cases, grammar (like voseo). HolaRuta's country guides show what's specific to each destination.

faq.question: Will Spain Spanish be understood in Latin America?
faq.answer: Yes, Spain Spanish is understood everywhere in Latin America - but sounding and vocabulary will clearly mark you as a foreigner, and some words differ enough to cause confusion (e.g. "coger" is neutral in Spain but vulgar in several Latin American countries). HolaRuta teaches the Latin American versions so you avoid exactly these mix-ups.

faq.question: Does HolaRuta teach Spain Spanish or Latin American Spanish?
faq.answer: HolaRuta teaches Latin-America-correct Spanish throughout - the words and phrases travellers actually hear on the ground, not textbook Castilian Spanish.

h1: How does spaced repetition work in HolaRuta?
question: How does spaced repetition work in HolaRuta?
intro: HolaRuta uses a simplified SM-2 algorithm: after every rating, the app recalculates when a card should resurface most efficiently - cards you know well repeat less often; cards you forget come back sooner.

sections.heading: How a review works in HolaRuta
bullets: HolaRuta shows you a due flashcard (question in German/English, answer in Spanish).
You rate it honestly: "Again" (wrong/forgotten), "Good" (correct but effortful), or "Easy" (knew it instantly).
HolaRuta's algorithm adjusts the interval until the next review: "Again" resets it, "Good"/"Easy" extend it.
The card automatically resurfaces on the calculated day - never too early, never too late.

sections.heading: What's happening under the hood?
paragraphs: In HolaRuta, every card carries a learning state made of an ease factor, an interval (in days), a due date and a repetition count. The ease factor is clamped to a sensible range so cards never become endlessly easy or endlessly hard.

faq.question: What is spaced repetition?
faq.answer: Spaced repetition is a learning technique where reviews happen at growing intervals instead of bluntly repeating everything over and over. HolaRuta implements this with a simplified SM-2 algorithm.

faq.question: Why do I forget vocabulary if I don't use spaced repetition?
faq.answer: Without scheduled review, newly learned material fades quickly (the "forgetting curve"). HolaRuta reviews a card right when you're about to forget it, which cements it long-term instead of letting it fade.

faq.question: Do I have to study in HolaRuta every day?
faq.answer: No, but regular, short sessions work best with spaced repetition - HolaRuta only shows you the cards that are actually due each day.

h1: ¿Qué es HolaRuta · Inglés?
question: ¿Qué es HolaRuta · Inglés?
intro: HolaRuta · Inglés es la versión invertida de HolaRuta: en vez de turistas aprendiendo español, es para trabajadores de hostelería y turismo en Latinoamérica que necesitan inglés para atender a huéspedes extranjeros - recepcionistas, meseros, guías turísticos y taxistas.

sections.heading: ¿Para quién es HolaRuta · Inglés?
paragraphs: HolaRuta · Inglés está pensado para personal de hostelería y turismo: recepción, meseros, guías, taxistas y cualquier persona que atienda turistas extranjeros y necesite inglés práctico para el trabajo, no inglés de salón de clases.

sections.heading: ¿Qué incluye?
bullets: 3124 tarjetas de inglés organizadas por situación real de trabajo (recepción, restaurante, quejas, entrevistas de trabajo).
Pronunciación adaptada para hispanohablantes (ej. 'de NIÚS-pei-per' para 'the newspaper').
Repetición espaciada: HolaRuta programa cada tarjeta para repetirse justo antes de que la olvides.
Gratis, sin cuenta obligatoria, funciona sin conexión a internet.

faq.question: ¿HolaRuta · Inglés es gratis?
faq.answer: Sí. HolaRuta · Inglés es completamente gratuito, sin suscripción ni costos ocultos.

faq.question: ¿Necesito una cuenta para usar HolaRuta · Inglés?
faq.answer: No. HolaRuta · Inglés funciona sin cuenta ni inicio de sesión; tu progreso se guarda localmente en tu dispositivo.

faq.question: ¿HolaRuta · Inglés funciona sin internet?
faq.answer: Sí. HolaRuta · Inglés es una aplicación web instalable (PWA) que funciona completamente sin conexión después de la primera instalación.

meta.title: Inglés para hostelería y turismo: todas las guías | HolaRuta

h1: Inglés para hostelería y turismo
question: ¿Qué guías de inglés para el trabajo ofrece HolaRuta?
intro: HolaRuta · Inglés organiza el inglés para el trabajo en turismo y hostelería por temas reales: recepción, restaurante, quejas de huéspedes, entrevistas de trabajo. Cada guía trae el vocabulario exacto que necesitas en ese momento.

meta.description: Estas 24 palabras y frases de inglés para "En el restaurante" te ayudan a atender a huéspedes y turistas en tu trabajo - vocabulario real, extraído del…

h1: En el restaurante: vocabulario de inglés para el trabajo
question: ¿Qué necesito saber de inglés para "En el restaurante"?
intro: Estas 24 palabras y frases de inglés para "En el restaurante" te ayudan a atender a huéspedes y turistas en tu trabajo - vocabulario real, extraído del corpus de tarjetas de HolaRuta · Inglés.

sections.heading: Frases esenciales
bullets: "¿Están listos para ordenar?" - "Are you ready to order?" (ar yu RE-di tu OR-der)
"¿Algo de tomar?" - "Anything to drink?" (E-ni-zing tu drink)
"¿Para comer aquí o para llevar?" - "For here or to go?" (for HIR or tu góu)
"Aquí tiene el menú." - "Here is the menu." (HIR is da MÉ-niu)
"¿Desea algún postre?" - "Would you like any dessert?" (wud yu laik É-ni di-SÉRT)
"Enseguida le traigo la cuenta." - "I'll bring you the bill right away." (ail bring yu da bil rait a-WÉI)
"Disculpe la demora." - "Sorry for the wait." (SO-rri for da wéit)
"¿Mesa para cuántos?" - "Table for how many?" (TÉI-bol for jau MÉ-ni)
"¿Todo bien por aquí?" - "Is everything okay here?" (is É-vri-zing o-KÉI jir)
"¿Cómo estuvo todo?" - "How was everything?" (jau was É-vri-zing)
"Aquí tiene sus cubiertos." - "Here are your utensils." (jir ar yor yu-TEN-sils)
"¿Le retiro el plato?" - "Can I take your plate?" (kan ai teik yor pleit)

sections.heading: Más vocabulario útil
bullets: "¿Cómo desea su bistec?" - "How would you like your steak?" (jau wud yu laik yor STÉIK)
"¿Tiene alguna alergia?" - "Do you have any allergies?" (du yu jav É-ni Á-ler-yis)
"El plato del día es pescado." - "Today's special is fish." (tu-DÉIS SPÉ-shal is fish)
"El baño está al fondo." - "The restroom is in the back." (da RÉST-rum is in da BAK)
"¿Le caliento la comida?" - "Shall I heat up your food?" (shal ai JIT op yor FUD)
"¿Desea ver la carta de vinos?" - "Would you like the wine list?" (wud yu laik da WÁIN list)

"Si gusta, puedo recomendarle algo ligero antes del plato fuerte." - "If you'd like, I can recommend something light before the main course." (if yud laik, ai kan re-co-MEND SOM-zing lait bi-FOR de mein cors)
"Le habría traído el plato antes, pero la cocina estaba llena." - "I would have brought your dish sooner, but the kitchen was swamped." (ai wud jav brot yor dish SU-ner, bot de KÍ-chen wos suampt)

"Si tiene prisa, puedo pedir que le preparen algo rápido." - "If you're in a hurry, I can ask the kitchen to prepare something quick." (if yur in a JÓ-rri, ai kan ask de KÍ-chen tu pri-PER SOM-zing kuik)
"Como es la primera vez que nos visita, le invito un aperitivo." - "Since it's your first time with us, the appetizer is on the house." (sins its yor ferst taim wiz os, di Á-pe-tai-ser is on de jaus)
"¿Le sirvo más pan mientras espera?" - "Would you like more bread while you wait?" (wud yu laik mor bred wail yu weit)
"¿Prefiere que le separe la cuenta?" - "Would you like me to split the bill?" (wud yu laik mi tu split de bil)

question: ¿Qué necesito saber de inglés para "Comuna 13"?
intro: 16 palabras y frases de inglés para dominar "Comuna 13" con huéspedes y turistas - directo del corpus real de HolaRuta · Inglés, sin relleno de manual.

sections.heading: Frases esenciales
bullets: "¡Bienvenidos a la Comuna 13! Soy su guía local." - "Welcome to Comuna 13! I'm your local guide." (WÉL-com tu ko-MÍU-na zir-TÍN aim yor LÓU-cal gaid.)
"Nací y crecí aquí en San Javier." - "I was born and raised here in San Javier." (ai uós born and réisd jir in san ja-VÍER.)
"Estas son las famosas escaleras eléctricas al aire libre." - "These are the famous outdoor escalators." (díis ar de FÉI-mas ÁUT-dor ÉS-ca-léi-tors.)
"Desde este mirador tienen la mejor vista para las fotos." - "From this viewpoint you get the best view for photos." (from dis VIÚ-point yu guet de best viú for FÓU-tos.)
"¿Quieren probar un mango biche o una paleta?" - "Would you like to try some green mango or a popsicle?" (uud yu laik tu trai sam gríin MÁN-gou or a POP-si-col)
"Síganme y tengan cuidado con el escalón." - "Follow me and watch your step." (FÓ-lou mi and uách yor step.)

sections.heading: Más vocabulario útil
bullets: "Hace años, este era uno de los barrios más peligrosos de Medellín." - "Years ago, this was one of the most dangerous neighborhoods in Medellín." (yírs a-GÓU, dis uós uán ov de moust DÉIN-yer-us NÉI-bor-juds in me-de-YÍN.)
"El barrio se transformó por completo gracias a su gente." - "The neighborhood transformed completely thanks to its people." (de NÉI-bor-jud trans-FORMD com-PLÍT-li zanks tu its PÍ-pol.)

"Fueron las primeras escaleras eléctricas públicas al aire libre construidas en un barrio popular del mundo." - "They were the first outdoor public escalators built in a low-income neighborhood in the world." (déi uér de ferst ÁUT-dor PÁ-blik ÉS-ca-léi-tors bilt in a lóu-ÍN-kam NÉI-bor-jud in de uérld.)
"Cada grafiti cuenta una historia real del barrio." - "Every piece of graffiti tells a real story about the neighborhood." (É-vri píis ov gra-FÍ-ti tels a ríil STÓO-ri a-BÁUT de NÉI-bor-jud.)

"Este mural representa a las madres que perdieron a sus hijos en la violencia." - "This mural represents the mothers who lost their children during the violence." (dis MIÚ-ral re-pri-SÉNTS de MÁ-ders ju lost der CHÍL-dren DIÚ-ring de VÁI-lens.)
"El hip-hop, el rap y el breakdance son el alma de la Comuna 13." - "Hip-hop, rap, and breakdancing are the soul of Comuna 13." (jip-jop, rap, and bréik-dan-sing ar de SÓUL ov ko-MÍU-na zir-TÍN.)
"Los jóvenes cambiaron las balas por el arte y la música." - "The young people traded bullets for art and music." (de yang PÍ-pol TRÉI-ded BÚ-lets for art and MIÚ-sic.)

"Si pueden, apoyen a los artistas locales comprándoles algo." - "If you can, please support the local artists by buying something from them." (if yu can, plíis su-PORT de LÓU-cal ÁR-tists bai BÁI-ing SAM-zing from dem.)
"Por favor, sean respetuosos: aquí vive gente de verdad." - "Please be respectful. Real people live here." (plíis bi ris-PÉKT-ful. ríil PÍ-pol liv jir.)

"Traigan protector solar, agua y zapatos cómodos; vamos a caminar bastante." - "Bring sunscreen, water, and comfortable shoes, because we're going to walk a lot." (bring SAN-scriin, UÓ-ter, and CÁMF-ta-bol shúus, bi-CÓS uir GÓU-ing tu uók a lot.)

sections.heading: Frases esenciales
bullets: "Bienvenido al Metro de Medellín. ¿En qué le puedo ayudar?" - "Welcome to the Medellín Metro! How can I help you?" (WEL-kum tu de meh-deh-YEEN MEH-tro. jáo kan ai jelp yu)
"Primero necesita comprar una tarjeta Cívica." - "First you need to buy a Cívica card." (FÉRST yu niid tu bái a SÍ-vi-ka kard)
"Puede recargar la tarjeta en la taquilla o en las máquinas." - "You can top up your card at the ticket booth or at the machines." (yu kan táp ap yor kard at de TÍ-ket buuz or at de ma-SHÍINS)
"Pase la tarjeta por aquí, sobre el lector." - "Tap your card here, on the reader." (táp yor kard jíir, on de RÍI-der)
"Hay dos líneas de tren: la Línea A y la Línea B." - "There are two train lines: Line A and Line B." (der ar tuu tréin láins: láin ei and láin bii)
"El transbordo es gratis, todo está integrado." - "The transfer is free — everything is integrated." (de TRÁNS-fer is fríi, ÉV-ri-zing is ÍN-te-grei-ted)

sections.heading: Más vocabulario útil
bullets: "Bájese en San Antonio y haga el transbordo a la otra línea." - "Get off at San Antonio and transfer to the other line." (get óf at san an-TÓ-nio and TRÁNS-fer tu de Á-der láin)
"Tome la Línea K del Metrocable para subir a Santo Domingo." - "Take Metrocable Line K up to Santo Domingo." (téik MEH-tro-kei-bl láin kéi ap tu SÁN-to do-MÍN-go)
"Para ir al Parque Arví, tome la Línea L desde Santo Domingo." - "To reach Parque Arví, take Line L from Santo Domingo." (tu ríich PAR-ke ar-VÍI, téik láin el from SÁN-to do-MÍN-go)
"Para la Comuna 13, bájese en San Javier y suba en la Línea J." - "For Comuna 13, get off at San Javier and go up on Line J." (for ko-MÚ-na zer-TÍIN, get óf at san ja-VÍER and góu ap on láin yéi)
"El Tranvía de Ayacucho conecta con las líneas H y M del cable." - "The Ayacucho Tram connects with cable Lines H and M." (de a-ya-KÚ-cho tram ko-NÉKTS wid KÉI-bl láins éich and em)
"Desde el Metrocable la vista de la ciudad es increíble." - "From the Metrocable, the view of the city is incredible." (from de MEH-tro-kei-bl, de viú of de SÍ-ti is in-KRÉ-di-bl)

"Aquí seguimos la 'Cultura Metro': mantenemos todo limpio y ordenado." - "Here we follow "Cultura Metro": we keep everything clean and orderly." (jíir wi FÓ-lou kul-TÚ-ra MEH-tro: wi kíip ÉV-ri-zing klíin and ÓR-der-li)

"Por favor, no coma ni beba, y ceda el asiento a los mayores y embarazadas." - "Please don't eat or drink, and give your seat to the elderly and pregnant women." (plíis dont íit or drink, and giv yor síit tu de EL-der-li and PREG-nant WÍ-men)

"Haga la fila y deje salir antes de entrar; nada de música a alto volumen." - "Form a line and let people out before you get on — no loud music." (form a láin and let PÍI-pl áut bi-FÓR yu get on, nóu láud MIÚ-sik)

"El Metro es seguro y limpio, y para nosotros es un orgullo paisa." - "The Metro is safe and clean, and for us it's a point of paisa pride." (de MEH-tro is séif and klíin, and for as its a póint of PÁI-sa práid)

h1: Medio ambiente: vocabulario de inglés para el trabajo
question: ¿Qué necesito saber de inglés para "Medio ambiente"?
intro: Para "Medio ambiente" en el trabajo necesitas estas 16 palabras y frases de inglés - vocabulario real del corpus de tarjetas de HolaRuta · Inglés, listo para practicar.

sections.heading: Frases esenciales
bullets: "¿Qué son los Corredores Verdes?" - "The Green Corridors are strips of trees and plants that Medellín planted along the main avenues and streams." (de grín KO-rri-dors ar strips of tríis and plants)
"¿Para qué sirven los corredores verdes?" - "They lowered the city temperature by about two degrees and gave us cleaner, cooler air." (dei LÓ-uerd de SÍ-ti TEM-pra-cher bai a-BÁUT tú di-GRÍIS)
"¿Qué es el 'pico y placa'?" - "It's a driving restriction based on your plate number, used to cut down traffic and pollution." (its a DRÁI-ving res-TRÍK-shon béisd on iór pléit)
"¿Aquí reciclan?" - "Yes, we separate the waste — we recycle paper, plastic, and glass here." (iés uí SE-pa-reit de uéist — uí ri-SÁI-kol)
"¿Dónde tiro la basura?" - "Please don't litter — there are bins over there for the different types of waste." (plíis dont LÍ-ter — der ar bins Ó-ver der)

sections.heading: Más vocabulario útil
bullets: "¿Es buena la calidad del aire aquí?" - "It's getting better, but in the dry season the air quality can drop, so keep an eye on the alerts." (its GUE-ding BE-ter bat in de drái SÍ-son)

"Cuéntame sobre el Río Medellín." - "The Medellín River used to be really polluted, but the city has been cleaning it up and building parks along it." (de me-de-LLÍN RÍ-ver iúsd tu bi RÍ-li po-LÚ-ted)
"¿Quién nos da el agua y la luz?" - "EPM, our public utility, provides the water and a lot of clean energy from hydropower." (e-pe-Éme aur PÁ-blik iu-TÍ-li-ti pro-VÁIDS de UÓ-ter)
"¿Puedo tomar el agua de la llave?" - "Yes, the tap water is safe, so you can refill your bottle instead of buying plastic ones." (iés de tap UÓ-ter is séif, so iu kan ri-FÍL iór BÓ-tol)
"¿Qué puedo visitar que sea verde?" - "You should visit the Botanical Garden and Parque Arví — they're like the green lungs of the city." (iu shud VÍ-sit de bo-TÁ-ni-kol GAR-den and PAR-ke ar-VÍ)
"¿El Metro contamina mucho?" - "No, the Metro is electric, so it's a really clean way to get around the city." (no de ME-tro is e-LÉK-trik, so its a RÍ-li klíin uéi)
"¿Hay bicicletas públicas?" - "Yes, we have EnCicla, a free public bike system — it's a great eco-friendly way to move around." (iés uí hav en-SÍ-kla, a fríi PÁ-blik báik SÍS-tem)
"¿Plantaron muchos árboles?" - "Yes, the city has planted thousands of trees and lots of plants, and you can really feel the difference." (iés de SÍ-ti has PLÁN-ted ZÁU-sands of tríis)

"¿Por qué hace menos calor en las avenidas con árboles?" - "The corridors create shade and release moisture, so they cool down whole stretches of the city." (de KO-rri-dors kri-ÉIT shéid and ri-LÍIS MÓIS-cher)

"¿Qué pasa cuando hay alerta por la calidad del aire?" - "When there's an air-quality alert, they tighten the driving restrictions and ask people to leave the car at home." (uén ders an er-KUÓ-li-ti a-LÉRT, dei TÁI-ten de DRÁI-ving)

"¿Cómo puedo ayudar a cuidar la ciudad mientras estoy aquí?" - "Honestly, just don't litter, refill your water bottle, and take the Metro or a bike whenever you can." (Ó-nest-li, llast dont LÍ-ter, ri-FÍL iór UÓ-ter BÓ-tol)

h1: Cultura paisa: vocabulario de inglés para el trabajo
question: ¿Qué necesito saber de inglés para "Cultura paisa"?
intro: 16 palabras y frases de inglés para dominar "Cultura paisa" con huéspedes y turistas - directo del corpus real de HolaRuta · Inglés, sin relleno de manual.

sections.heading: Frases esenciales
bullets: "¿Cómo le explico a un turista qué significa 'parce' o 'parcero'?" - "'Parce' or 'parcero' just means buddy or friend. We say it all the time here." (PAR-say / PAR-say-ro. Say 'BUH-dee', roll the 'r' a little in parce.)

"Un turista escuchó '¡qué chimba!' y quiere saber qué es. ¿Cómo lo explico con cuidado?" - "'Qué chimba' means how cool or awesome — but it's slang and a bit crude, so use it with friends, not with your grandma." (keh CHEEM-ba. Say 'kool' and 'AH-sum'; 'crude' like 'krood'.)
"¿Cómo traduzco '¡qué nota!' cuando algo me encanta?" - "'Qué nota' means that's awesome or that's great — we say it when we really love something." (keh NO-ta. 'awesome': AH-sum, 'great': grayt.)

"Un visitante pregunta qué es 'bacano' o 'chévere'. ¿Cómo lo digo en inglés?" - "'Bacano' and 'chévere' both mean cool or nice — a friendly way to say we like something." (ba-KA-no / CHEH-ve-re. 'cool': kool, 'nice': nice.)
"¿Qué le digo si oye a alguien exclamar '¡eh, Ave María!'?" - "'Eh, Ave María' is a classic paisa expression of surprise — like saying oh my goodness or wow." (o mai GUD-nes, or uáu. sur-PRÁIZ.)

"Quiero hablarle de los arrieros, nuestros antepasados muleros. ¿Cómo lo digo?" - "Our ancestors were 'arrieros' — muleteers who crossed the mountains with their mules. That heritage shaped who we are." ('ancestors': AN-ses-torz. 'mountains': MOWN-tinz. 'heritage': HEH-ri-tij.)

sections.heading: Más vocabulario útil
bullets: "El turista quiere lanzarse a algo y oye 'hágale' o 'de una'. ¿Cómo lo explico?" - "'Hágale' or 'de una' means go for it or let's do it — it's how we say yes with energy." ('go for it' — say 'for' softly. 'lets DOO it'.)

"¿Cómo le explico qué es un 'parche' cuando lo invito a salir?" - "A 'parche' is a hangout or a group of friends — so 'vamos de parche' means let's go hang out together." (HANG-out. 'together': tu-GEH-der (the 'th' sounds like a soft 'd').)

"Quiero contarle sobre la 'berraquera' paisa, nuestro empuje. ¿Cómo lo digo?" - "'Berraquera' is our word for grit and hard work — a 'berraco' is someone tough and determined who never gives up." (grit: grit. 'determined': di-TER-mind. 'though'-free 'tough': tuff.)

"El turista pide un café. ¿Cómo le explico qué es un 'tinto'?" - "A 'tinto' is a small black coffee — no milk. We drink them all day long here in coffee country." ('black': blak, 'coffee': KAW-fee. 'though' not needed — say 'all day'.)

"Después de almorzar nos quedamos charlando. ¿Cómo explico la 'sobremesa'?" - "The 'sobremesa' is when we stay at the table after eating, just talking and enjoying each other's company." ('the': de. 'table': TAY-bul. 'company': KUM-pa-nee.)

"A veces la gente lo llamará 'mi amor' o 'mijo'. ¿Cómo le aclaro que es cariño?" - "Don't be surprised if people call you 'mi amor' or 'mijo' — it's just a warm, friendly way we treat everyone." ('warm': worm, 'friendly': FREND-lee. 'everyone': EV-ree-wun.)

"Un turista escucha 'sumercé' y le parece muy formal. ¿Cómo se lo explico?" - "'Sumercé' is an old, very respectful and tender way of saying you — you'll hear it more in central Colombia than here in Medellín." (sú-mer-SÉ is an óuld, ri-SPEKT-ful uéi of SÉI-ing yu.)

"Le muestro un pueblo paisa con balcones de colores. ¿Cómo describo eso?" - "These are our 'pueblos paisas' — little towns with white walls and colorful balconies full of flowers." ('towns': townz, 'colorful': KUH-lor-ful. 'flowers': FLOW-erz.)

"Quiero que el turista se sienta como en casa. ¿Cómo le digo 'está en su casa'?" - "Please make yourself at home — here you're family, and our door is always open to you." ('yourself': yor-SELF, 'family': FAM-i-lee. 'always': AWL-wayz.)

"Al despedirme quiero decirle que siempre será bienvenido. ¿Cómo lo digo con calidez?" - "You're always welcome here — come back anytime, and we'll be so happy to see you again." ('welcome': WEL-kum, 'anytime': EH-nee-time. 'again': a-GEN.)

h1: Comida paisa: vocabulario de inglés para el trabajo
question: ¿Qué necesito saber de inglés para "Comida paisa"?
intro: Estas 16 palabras y frases de inglés para "Comida paisa" te ayudan a atender a huéspedes y turistas en tu trabajo - vocabulario real, extraído del corpus de tarjetas de HolaRuta · Inglés.

sections.heading: Frases esenciales
bullets: "¿Qué es la bandeja paisa?" - "The bandeja paisa is our biggest traditional dish. It's a large platter with lots of different things on one plate." (BIG-est tra-DISH-uh-nul DISH; PLA-ter)

"¿Qué trae la bandeja paisa?" - "It comes with red beans, white rice, ground beef, fried pork belly, a fried egg, chorizo, sweet plantain, avocado, and an arepa." (red BEENZ; ground BEEF; FRIED pork BEH-lee; a-vo-CAH-do)
"¿Qué es el chicharrón?" - "Chicharrón is fried pork belly. It's crispy on the outside and really tasty. Most people love it." (chee-cha-RON; CRIS-pee; TAY-stee)
"¿Qué es una arepa aquí en Medellín?" - "Here our arepa is a plain, thick corn cake. It has no filling, so it's not like the arepas from other regions." (PLAIN, ZICK corn CAKE; NO FILL-ing)
"¿Qué son los frijoles paisas?" - "The frijoles are red beans slow-cooked with pork. We eat them almost every day, and they're the heart of the bandeja paisa." (red BEENZ; SLOW-cookt; HART)
"¿Cómo es el desayuno típico paisa?" - "A typical breakfast is arepa with eggs, or calentado, which is rice and beans from the day before, warmed up again." (TIP-ih-kul BREK-fust; WARMD UP a-GEN)

sections.heading: Más vocabulario útil
bullets: "Le recomiendo la bandeja paisa si tiene mucha hambre." - "I'd recommend the bandeja paisa if you're really hungry. It's a huge plate and it has a bit of everything." (reh-koh-MEND; HYOOJ PLATE; a BIT of EV-ree-zing)
"¿Qué es el sancocho?" - "Sancocho is a hearty soup with meat, plantain, yuca, potato and corn. It's comfort food, perfect for lunch." (san-KOH-cho; HAR-tee SOUP; KUM-fort food)
"¿Qué es el mondongo?" - "Mondongo is a tripe soup, made with beef stomach and vegetables. It's very traditional, but not for everyone." (mon-DON-go; TRYPE soup; STUH-muk)
"¿Las empanadas pican?" - "No, the empanadas aren't spicy. They come with ají on the side, and you add as much as you like." (AREN'T SPY-see; ah-HEE on de SIDE)
"¿Me recomienda algo dulce para acompañar?" - "Try a buñuelo, a fried cheese dough ball, or pandebono, a cheese bread. Both go great with coffee." (boo-NYWEH-lo; CHEEZ BREAD; go GRATE wid KOH-fee)
"¿Qué es la mazamorra?" - "Mazamorra is a cold drink of cooked corn in milk. We serve it with panela, which is raw cane sugar, to sweeten it." (mah-sah-MOR-ah; RAW CANE SHUH-gar; SWEE-ten)

"¿Qué bebidas típicas tenemos?" - "You can try aguardiente, our strong anise liquor, a local beer like Águila or Club Colombia, or a tinto, a small black coffee." (STRONG AN-iss LIH-ker; TIN-to; SMALL BLACK KOH-fee)
"¿Qué jugos naturales tienen?" - "We have fresh fruit juices made to order, like lulo, passion fruit, or mango. They're really refreshing." (FRESH FROOT JOO-siz; PASH-un froot; ree-FRESH-ing)
"¿La comida paisa es picante?" - "No, paisa food is generally not spicy. If you want some heat, we bring ají to the table so you can add it yourself." (NOT SPY-see; if you WANT sum HEET; ah-HEE)

"¿Tiene alguna restricción alimentaria o alergia?" - "Do you have any dietary restrictions or allergies? Just so you know, the portions here are very large." (DY-eh-teh-ree ree-STRIK-shunz; AL-er-jeez; POR-shunz)

faq.answer: En inglés: "The bandeja paisa is our biggest traditional dish. It's a large platter with lots of different things on one plate." (pronunciación: BIG-est tra-DISH-uh-nul DISH; PLA-ter). Puedes practicar esta frase como tarjeta en la app HolaRuta · Inglés.
